text: Hello, I'm sharing an extended right colectomy.
This is a patient who on an original colonoscopy was found to have four separate right-sided colon cancers.
They were all very small polyps, between one and two centimeters, but all positive for well-differentiated adeno.

all completely removed with negative margins and the most distal polyp was in the proximal transverse colon and a tattoo was placed distal to that there were no distal polyps and after genetic testing and consultation the patient opted for a colectomy as definitive management.
The surgeon ligates and divides the ileocolic pedicle, isolating it from surrounding tissues during the extended right colectomy.

This is an elderly individual. I did not necessarily think they would benefit from a CME as well as the fact that the tumors were all well differentiated, very small and completely resected with endoscopy.
The surgical team navigates over the head of the pancreas, approaching the right colic vessels, with visible anatomical structures and surrounding tissue.
So some of the times when I do extended rights, I do a more transverse orientation of the ports. I did my standard left upper to suprapubic port position for this case and it worked out for me.
The surgeon port hops the camera from port three to port two to facilitate dissection over the transverse colon, enhancing the visual field for precise surgical manipulation.

The surgeon identifies the tattoo placed distal to the most distal cancer, marking the intended site for mesenteric transection from the middle colic region up to the margin. The right colic vessels are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds to transect the right colic vessels, which have been exposed during the dissection.
The surgeon approaches the middle colic vessels, which are located near the previously identified right colic vessels, as the dissection progresses over the transverse colon.
The surgeon points out the visible vein adjacent to the dissected area, highlighting its location in the surgical field.
The surgeon ligates and transects the middle colic vein, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the middle colic artery, visible in the surgical field, as part of the ongoing dissection process.
The surgeon approaches the planned distal transection margin, cutting through the mesentery to connect with the area where the middle colic vessels were previously taken.
The surgeon demonstrates the line of dissection by placing the vessel sealer behind the middle colic vessels, highlighting the area for transection.
The surgeon addresses the attachments of the mesentery, preparing to proceed with further dissection.

The surgeon switches the camera view and continues dissecting the small bowel mesentery, focusing on the inferior aspect. The surgeon operates with two instruments in the right hand and one in the left.
The surgeon joins the medially dissected plane, connecting the previously dissected areas within the mesentery.
This is kind of out of order for me in terms of how I normally progress with my surgeries, but I had good exposure to it when I first port hopped, so I just did it.
The surgeon returns to the transverse colon and begins splitting the omentum over the planned resection margin, preparing to resect the omentum along with the specimen.
The surgeon proceeds through the gastrocolic omentum, entering the lesser sac proper, revealing the anatomical space between the stomach and transverse colon.
The surgeon navigates around the lateral attachments of the colon, with a visible sponge marking the area of previous medial dissection.
The surgeon navigates around the hepatic flexure, continuing the dissection in the area where the colon curves near the liver.
The surgeon detaches the final attachments of the right colon, joining the area of previous inferior dissection, resulting in the complete mobilization of the colon.
The surgeon positions the camera in arm two and staples the distal transverse colon at the suprapubic region, preparing for resection.
The surgeon staples the small bowel, using a surgical stapler to secure the tissue.

The surgeon positions the stapler at the stapled end of the colon to perform an isoparastaltic anastomosis, ensuring the stapler fits properly from this angle rather than approaching from the small bowel end.
The surgeon proceeds to sew the closed end of the anastomosis, completing the closure of the surgical site.

The final pathology proved that there was actually no residual carcinoma in the right colon. There were a couple of other small adenomas in the right colon and the margins were clean. And the patient did very well and happy with their decision. And I hope you enjoyed.
